title: Human Capital Is Being Demonetized — And Your Law Degree Won't Save You

source.original: You Have 10 Years to Join the AI Economy | Michael Saylor

body_html:
For generations, the path to prosperity was clear: acquire skills, earn credentials, work hard, and exchange your labor for money. Michael Saylor argues that this entire model is being systematically dismantled. White-collar AI bots are already here, automating legal drafting, medical opinions, financial analysis, and contract composition. Within a decade, robotics will do the same to blue-collar work — painting walls, moving furniture, installing doors, driving vehicles. The marginal cost of human labor is being driven toward zero.

The implications are stark. A £200,000 law degree may be worth less than £200,000 of desirable property. When everyone can access 10,000 AI lawyers for £10 a month, the value of being the person who drafts the contract evaporates. The same logic applies to doctors, accountants, engineers, and virtually every profession built on specialized knowledge that can be encoded. Saylor compares it to the agricultural revolution: no matter how strong or hardworking you are, you're not more productive than a tractor.

What retains value in this new landscape isn't talent or hard work — it's distribution, influence, and network ownership. Someone with 5 million followers can monetize an AI-generated video instantly. The person who painstakingly created it by hand but has 50 followers cannot. Apple can deploy any AI to a billion devices because it owns the distribution channel. Amazon can capitalize on self-driving delivery because it already has last-mile access to hundreds of millions of households.

Saylor's advice for anyone starting their career is pointed: do something nobody has ever done before, using tools that were never available to your parents. Trying to replicate your parents' career path with their methods and their tools is a recipe for obsolescence. The winners of the next decade will be those who stake claims in digital networks, build audiences, and own assets — not those who accumulate credentials and sell their time.

The world won't lack prosperity. A billion robots will produce infinite goods, entertainment, and services. But the abundance will be socialized through entitlement programs while the ownership of scarce assets — the land, the networks, the protocols — will determine who actually builds wealth. The clock is ticking, and the window to position yourself is roughly ten years.
